original_abstract: The advancement of parallel convolution technologies has significantly enhanced the complexity and functionality of optical neural networks (ONNs) by leveraging the wavelength dimension. Despite these gains, high-level integration and efficient on-chip operation remain formidable challenges. In this work, we demonstrate a microcomb-driven photonic processing unit (PPU) that implements convolution using a time-wavelength plane stretching approach. By developing a dedicated control and operation protocol to support the PPU, we achieve a record-breaking weight precision of 9 bits. The system's compact architecture and rapid data-loading capabilities enable a landmark photonic-core compute density exceeding 1 trillion operations per second per square millimeter (TOPS mm⁻²). We validate the platform through two proof-of-concept experiments—image edge detection and handwritten digit recognition—attaining performance levels comparable to digital electronic computers. Given its superior performance and inherent scalability, this parallel photonic processing unit offers a transformative path for demanding artificial intelligence tasks, such as autonomous driving, real-time video action recognition, and high-fidelity image reconstruction.
humanized_abstract: Following on from our recent publication about parallel convolution in the wavelength domain (i.e., using optical neural networks - ONNs), we demonstrated the challenges of highly integrating such architecture, as well as on-chip efficient data access. In a new paper published in Optica (IOP) this week, we discuss a breakthrough microcomb technology-based photonic processing unit that could make possible fast and efficient convolutions at the optics level, albeit by using a new approach where we utilize a time-wavelength plane stretching similar to Optical Fourier Transform approach. We also proposed a specific method of controlling this new PPU and devised an operating protocol to ensure that the resulting high degree of weight precision that we could achieve - we attained a record 9 bits. Our PPU, henceforth termed micro-comb parallel photonic PPU (MC4) is highly integrated in the wavelength and time domain which leads to a highly attractive photonic-core compute density of over 1 trillion operations per second per square millimeter (TOPS mm⁻²) while we demonstrated at photonic-layer in two tests that the whole system acts in fact at a similar level of performance to a standard electronic computer (we implemented image edge detection as well as a simple version of a handwritten digit recognition, based on learned, pre-trained deep learning algorithms). Potential applications of MC4 include autonomous self-driving vehicles that perform instant action recognition of moving objects via real-time video processing. Another application could include instant classification in video analytics based on real-time action recognition in every frame, for example in smart CCTV cameras or more exotic use cases such as ultra-high-quality image enhancement of distant or low-light scenes from, for example space-based imaging or even the distant events in galaxy clusters.
